hook: You already know how to find the probability of an event — but what happens when you learn that something else has already occurred? A weather forecast changes when you look outside and see dark clouds; a medical test means more when you know a patient's history. In this unit, you'll discover how new information reshapes probability, unlocking tools that power everything from spam filters to medical diagnoses.
concept: The unifying idea is that probability is not fixed — it updates when we gain knowledge about related events. Formally, this is captured by the concept of *conditional probability*, $P(A \mid B)$, which threads through every topic in this unit.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to calculate $P(A \mid B)$, the probability that event $A$ occurs given that event $B$ has already happened, and interpret what it means to restrict your sample space.
Master the rule $P(A \cap B) = P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)$ to find the probability that two events both occur, chaining conditional probabilities together.
You will learn to identify when two events do not influence each other — so that $P(A \mid B) = P(A)$ — and use independence to simplify probability calculations.
Apply Bayes' Theorem to reverse conditional probabilities, answering questions like "given a positive test result, what is the actual probability of having the disease?"
You will learn to organize multi-stage probability problems visually with tree diagrams, making it straightforward to apply the multiplication rule and Bayes' Theorem without losing track of cases.
